01 / ABOUT

Engineering intent,
built into every part.

JEYABOOPATHI.S / MECH.DESIGN

I'm a Mechanical Design Engineer working at the intersection of robotics, industrial automation, and product development. My work starts with a real mechanical problem — a motion that needs to happen reliably, a structure that needs to hold up under load, a station that needs to move parts faster — and follows it through concept, CAD, simulation, and prototype.

I build in SolidWorks and Fusion 360, validate with simulation, and bring designs into the physical world through CNC machining, 3D printing, and hands-on prototyping. On the automation side, I work across PLC programming, pneumatics, and mechatronics to get mechanical systems talking to control systems — because a good mechanism is only half the job until it's automated.

I care about designs that are manufacturable, not just modelable — parts that survive contact with a machine shop, an assembly line, and an end user. That's the standard I hold every project to, from a single bracket to a full robotic workcell.

CAD & Product Design
Robotics & Mechatronics
PLC & Industrial Automation
Digital Manufacturing
Rapid Prototyping

03 / FEATURED WORK

Selected engineering projects.

From mechanism design to automated production cells — concept through prototype.

CAD.01
01

Caravan Roof Lifting Mechanism

A manually operated elevating-roof system engineered for smooth, synchronized lift with minimal user effort. The mechanism combines a worm gear reduction stage, bevel gear transmission, and ball-screw actuator, guided by LM linear rails to keep all four corners of the roof rising in sync.

  • Worm gear reduction mechanism
  • Bevel gear transmission
  • Ball screw actuator
  • LM guide rail synchronization
  • Scissor-lift support structure
  • Full CAD design & mechanical analysis
SolidWorksMotion StudyDFM
CAD.02
02

SCARA Robot Development

Design and development of a compact SCARA-class robotic arm, from mechanical structure through 3D-printed functional prototype. Link geometry and joint layout were iterated around payload capacity, reach, and repeatability, with motion analysis used to validate arm behaviour before committing to hardware.

  • Mechanical structure & joint design
  • 3D-printed prototype development
  • Payload optimization
  • Motion & reach analysis
Fusion 3603D PrintingKinematics
CAD.03
03

Industrial Automation Solutions

A modular three-station automation workcell built around pick-and-place handling, pneumatic actuation, and PLC-based sequencing. Each station is designed as an independent, swappable module with its own control panel — start/stop pushbuttons and status indicators — allowing the line to be reconfigured for different manufacturing processes.

  • Pick-and-place automation systems
  • Pneumatic actuation design
  • PLC-based control sequencing
  • Manufacturing process improvement
SolidWorksTIA PortalPneumatics
CAD.04
04

Digital Manufacturing & Reverse Engineering

A scan-to-CAD workflow that takes physical geometry — including fine detail like fluted columns and profiled mouldings — through 3D scanning into fully parametric CAD reconstruction, ready for rapid prototyping and small-batch manufacture.

  • 3D scanning & point-cloud capture
  • CAD reconstruction from scan data
  • Prototype development
  • Rapid manufacturing
3D ScanningSolidWorks3D Printing

04 / METHOD

How a mechanism gets built.

The same sequence, every project — from problem to part in hand.

Requirement Analysis

Define function, loads, constraints and success criteria.

Concept Development

Sketch and compare mechanism options against the brief.

CAD Design

Model parts and assemblies with manufacturing in mind.

Simulation

Validate motion, stress and fit before cutting metal.

Prototype Development

Build functional prototypes to prove the concept.

Testing

Verify performance, durability and repeatability.

Final Product

Release a design that's ready for the shop floor.
